title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear an instructor called Chloe talking about an outdoor activity centre. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear an instructor called Chloe talking about an outdoor activity centre. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: An instructor called Chloe describes courses at an outdoor activity centre.
extracts.audio_script: Hi everyone. I’m Chloe and I work at Hilltop Outdoor Activity Centre, where groups spend weekends trying new activities. Although our name suggests we are on a mountain, the surrounding land is gently hilly and suitable for beginners. The centre occupies buildings that once belonged to a [9]farm[/9]; the old barn is now the dining room, and the former fields are used for games. We welcome school and college groups, families and individual adults. Most visitors stay for two nights, arriving on Friday evening and leaving after lunch on Sunday.||Some people come expecting activities on the water, but our most popular first activity is on land. The [10]climbing wall[/10] is used on Saturday morning because instructors can quickly see how confident each person feels at height. It has routes for complete beginners as well as more challenging sections. All specialist safety equipment is supplied by the centre. Visitors often bring heavy coats, but what is really important for ordinary activities is a [11]waterproof jacket[/11], as plans continue in light rain. Trainers are suitable unless your group has arranged a longer hill walk.||On Saturday afternoon, groups choose between several sessions. Canoeing is available in warmer months, and archery can be arranged for older visitors. The session that all school groups must complete is [12]map reading[/12], because we want everyone to understand where they are and how to return safely. This begins indoors and finishes with a short route outdoors. Canoeing does not take place on the river, which has a strong current. Instead, it is offered on the small [13]lake[/13] behind the centre, where instructors can see everyone clearly from the shore.||Sleeping arrangements are simple but comfortable. Some visitors ask whether they may camp; we only permit that during special summer events. Weekend groups stay in [14]wooden cabins[/14], each sleeping up to six people, with bathrooms in a separate building nearby. Meals are served in the converted barn. Breakfast and the evening meal are included in the price, but for the daytime walk each visitor should bring a [15]packed lunch[/15]. Bottles can be refilled at the centre before you leave. We also offer an indoor option if conditions are poor: the most popular rainy-day activity last season was [16]photography[/16], using cameras to capture small natural details around the buildings.||People completing one weekend do not receive a qualification, but those who return for our longer leadership course can gain a [17]first-aid certificate[/17], which is useful for anyone working with young people. Finally, our centre has one major improvement planned. We thought about adding more bedrooms, but access across the stream is our priority. Money raised at this summer’s open day will help build a new [18]bridge[/18], making it easier for wheelchair users and families with small children to reach activity areas. I have booking information here for anyone interested.

dialogue.speaker: chloe
dialogue.text: Hi everyone. I’m Chloe and I work at Hilltop Outdoor Activity Centre, where groups spend weekends trying new activities. Although our name suggests we are on a mountain, the surrounding land is gently hilly and suitable for beginners. The centre occupies buildings that once belonged to a farm; the old barn is now the dining room, and the former fields are used for games. We welcome school and college groups, families and individual adults. Most visitors stay for two nights, arriving on Friday evening and leaving after lunch on Sunday.||Some people come expecting activities on the water, but our most popular first activity is on land. The climbing wall is used on Saturday morning because instructors can quickly see how confident each person feels at height. It has routes for complete beginners as well as more challenging sections. All specialist safety equipment is supplied by the centre. Visitors often bring heavy coats, but what is really important for ordinary activities is a waterproof jacket, as plans continue in light rain. Trainers are suitable unless your group has arranged a longer hill walk.||On Saturday afternoon, groups choose between several sessions. Canoeing is available in warmer months, and archery can be arranged for older visitors. The session that all school groups must complete is map reading, because we want everyone to understand where they are and how to return safely. This begins indoors and finishes with a short route outdoors. Canoeing does not take place on the river, which has a strong current. Instead, it is offered on the small lake behind the centre, where instructors can see everyone clearly from the shore.||Sleeping arrangements are simple but comfortable. Some visitors ask whether they may camp; we only permit that during special summer events. Weekend groups stay in wooden cabins, each sleeping up to six people, with bathrooms in a separate building nearby. Meals are served in the converted barn. Breakfast and the evening meal are included in the price, but for the daytime walk each visitor should bring a packed lunch. Bottles can be refilled at the centre before you leave. We also offer an indoor option if conditions are poor: the most popular rainy-day activity last season was photography, using cameras to capture small natural details around the buildings.||People completing one weekend do not receive a qualification, but those who return for our longer leadership course can gain a first-aid certificate, which is useful for anyone working with young people. Finally, our centre has one major improvement planned. We thought about adding more bedrooms, but access across the stream is our priority. Money raised at this summer’s open day will help build a new bridge, making it easier for wheelchair users and families with small children to reach activity areas. I have booking information here for anyone interested.
